news_summary: Recent health advancements reveal promising breakthroughs in addiction treatment, simplified effective strength training, surprising insights on Ozempic's effects, and innovative mitochondrial therapies for disease.
news_summary_fr: Les récentes avancées dans le domaine de la santé révèlent des percées prometteuses dans le traitement de la dépendance, des programmes de musculation simplifiés et efficaces, des découvertes surprenantes sur les effets de l'Ozempic, ainsi que des thérapies mitochondriales innovantes pour lutter contre certaines maladies.
news_summary_es: Los últimos avances en el ámbito de la salud revelan avances prometedores en el tratamiento de las adicciones, un entrenamiento de fuerza eficaz y simplificado, datos sorprendentes sobre los efectos de Ozempic y terapias mitocondriales innovadoras para el tratamiento de enfermedades.

articles.title: A single dose of psilocybin gave smokers six times better odds of quitting than the patch
articles.summary:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM A new clinical trial from Johns Hopkins University produced results that surprised even the researchers behind it. Participants who took a single dose of psilocybin, the active compound in magic mushrooms, had more than six times greater odds of being cigarette-free at the six-month mark compared to those using nicotine […]
The post A single dose of psilocybin gave smokers six times better odds of quitting than the patch first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM

A new clinical trial from Johns Hopkins University produced results that surprised even the researchers behind it. Participants who took a single dose of psilocybin, the active compound in magic mushrooms, had more than six times greater odds of being cigarette-free at the six-month mark compared to those using nicotine patches.

“I was surprised by the sheer magnitude of the effect,” said Matthew Johnson, the study’s lead author and a professor of psychiatry at Johns Hopkins.

What the trial actually looked like

The study enrolled 82 current smokers, randomly divided into two groups. Both went through 13 weeks of cognitive behavioral therapy for smoking cessation. One group used nicotine patches; the other took a relatively high dose of pure psilocybin in a single guided session.

Participants in the psilocybin group lay in a room wearing eye shades and listening to soft music, with facilitators present to keep them comfortable. The experience itself was “self-directed,” Johnson said. At six months, 17 people in the psilocybin group had stayed off cigarettes. Only four in the nicotine patch group had done the same.

There was no placebo in this trial. Blinding participants in psychedelic research is a known challenge given the obvious mind-altering effects, so the researchers opted for a different design. That’s a real limitation, and Johnson acknowledged the findings will need to be replicated in a larger, more diverse study before drawing firm conclusions. The National Institutes of Health has already funded him for exactly that: a larger ongoing trial that includes a placebo arm.

Why this is different from every existing quit-smoking tool

Seven medications are currently approved for smoking cessation in the United States. Most work by targeting nicotine receptors directly, essentially replacing or mimicking the substance someone is trying to quit.

Psilocybin takes a completely different route. “It’s a very different treatment approach from just trying to replace or sort of mimic the drug that’s being misused,” said addiction psychiatrist Dr. Brian Barnett of the Cleveland Clinic Lerner College of Medicine, who was not involved in the study.

Even with the best available medications and counseling combined, long-term quit rates hover between 20 and 30 percent per attempt. That means 70 percent of people return to smoking. “How do we help those folks?” asked Megan Piper, director of the UW Center for Tobacco Research and Intervention.

Brain imaging data from this latest study are still being analyzed, but Johnson described what appears to be happening at a behavioral level. “When people are on a compound like psilocybin, the brain is communicating with itself in very different ways,” he said. What follows, in conversations with past participants, seems to be a genuine shift in perspective and a new sense of agency. “Rather than falling into the same stories, these same patterns, it seems that things are shaken up and they can step outside of that and try something different.”

Barnett suspects the intensive therapeutic structure around the psilocybin session was also central to the outcome. “It’s not the drug by itself here. It’s really harnessing the neuroplastic and learning effects that happen after the exposure.”

A field gaining traction at the right moment

This study builds on a smaller pilot Johnson ran more than a decade ago. Together, the two sets of results were compelling enough to secure NIH funding for the next phase. Research into psilocybin for alcohol dependence has also shown early promise, and ibogaine is drawing serious attention for opioid use disorder.

Smoking remains the leading cause of preventable death in the United States. “It’s been 20 years since we’ve had a new medication to help people quit smoking,” Piper said. “We need something novel, and this is definitely a novel approach.”

The larger NIH-funded trial, which includes a placebo arm and a more diverse participant pool, will go a long way toward answering whether these results hold at scale. Brain imaging data from this study, still under analysis, may add more texture to the mechanism. The field has real momentum, and the questions worth asking now are getting sharper.

Source study: JAMA Network Open– Psilocybin or nicotine patch for smoking cessation a pilot randomised clinical trial

Did this solution stand out? Share it with a friend or support our mission by becoming an Emissary.
articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a significant clinical trial showing that a single dose of psilocybin greatly improves smoking cessation rates compared to nicotine patches, representing a novel and promising treatment approach for a major public health issue. The study has broad potential impact, is well contextualized, and is supported by ongoing NIH-funded research.
articles.personality_title: Single dose of psilocybin helps smokers quit six times more than nicotine patches
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Smoking is the leading cause of preventable death in the United States. Many people try to quit using nicotine patches or medications, but success rates are often low, with about 70% returning to smoking. Researchers have been searching for new ways to help smokers quit for good.

**What happened** – Johns Hopkins University conducted a clinical trial with 82 smokers. All participants received 13 weeks of therapy to help them quit smoking. One group used nicotine patches, while the other took a single high dose of psilocybin, a compound found in magic mushrooms. Six months later, 17 people in the psilocybin group had quit smoking, compared to only 4 in the patch group.

**Impact** – This study shows that psilocybin could be a powerful new tool for quitting smoking. Unlike nicotine patches, which replace nicotine, psilocybin seems to change how the brain works, helping people see their habits differently and feel more control over quitting. This is the first time a single dose of a psychedelic has shown such strong results in smoking cessation.

**What's next step** – The researchers are now running a larger study funded by the National Institutes of Health. This new study will include more participants from different backgrounds and a placebo group to confirm the results. They are also studying brain scans to better understand how psilocybin helps people quit.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A single guided dose of psilocybin combined with therapy helped smokers quit six times more often than nicotine patches in a new clinical trial.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une dose unique de psilocybine aide les fumeurs à arrêter six fois plus que les patchs nicotiniques
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le tabagisme est la principale cause de décès évitable aux États-Unis. Beaucoup de personnes essaient d’arrêter avec des patchs nicotiniques ou des médicaments, mais les taux de réussite restent faibles, environ 70 % recommencent à fumer. Les chercheurs cherchent de nouvelles méthodes pour aider à arrêter définitivement.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – L’université Johns Hopkins a mené un essai clinique avec 82 fumeurs. Tous ont suivi 13 semaines de thérapie pour arrêter de fumer. Un groupe a utilisé des patchs nicotiniques, l’autre a pris une seule dose élevée de psilocybine, un composé des champignons magiques. Six mois plus tard, 17 personnes du groupe psilocybine avaient arrêté de fumer, contre seulement 4 dans le groupe patch.

**Impact** – Cette étude montre que la psilocybine pourrait être un outil puissant pour arrêter de fumer. Contrairement aux patchs qui remplacent la nicotine, la psilocybine semble changer le fonctionnement du cerveau, aidant les personnes à voir leurs habitudes autrement et à mieux contrôler leur arrêt. C’est la première fois qu’une dose unique de psychédélique montre de tels résultats.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs mènent maintenant une étude plus grande financée par les Instituts nationaux de la santé. Cette nouvelle étude inclura plus de participants diversifiés et un groupe placebo pour confirmer les résultats. Ils étudient aussi des images cérébrales pour mieux comprendre le fonctionnement.

**En une phrase** – Une dose guidée unique de psilocybine associée à une thérapie a aidé des fumeurs à arrêter six fois plus que les patchs nicotiniques dans un nouvel essai clinique.
articles.personality_title_es: Una dosis única de psilocibina ayuda a fumadores a dejar el tabaco seis veces más que los parches de nicotina
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Fumar es la principal causa de muerte prevenible en Estados Unidos. Muchas personas intentan dejarlo usando parches de nicotina o medicamentos, pero las tasas de éxito son bajas, con un 70 % que vuelve a fumar. Los investigadores buscan nuevas formas de ayudar a dejar de fumar para siempre.

**Qué pasó** – La Universidad Johns Hopkins realizó un ensayo clínico con 82 fumadores. Todos recibieron 13 semanas de terapia para dejar de fumar. Un grupo usó parches de nicotina, mientras que el otro tomó una sola dosis alta de psilocibina, un compuesto de los hongos mágicos. A los seis meses, 17 personas del grupo de psilocibina habían dejado de fumar, comparado con solo 4 del grupo de parches.

**Impacto** – Este estudio muestra que la psilocibina podría ser una herramienta muy eficaz para dejar de fumar. A diferencia de los parches que reemplazan la nicotina, la psilocibina parece cambiar cómo funciona el cerebro, ayudando a las personas a ver sus hábitos de forma diferente y a tener más control para dejar el tabaco. Es la primera vez que una dosis única de un psicodélico muestra resultados tan fuertes.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores están realizando un estudio más grande financiado por los Institutos Nacionales de Salud. Este nuevo estudio incluirá más participantes de diferentes orígenes y un grupo placebo para confirmar los resultados. También están analizando imágenes cerebrales para entender mejor cómo ayuda la psilocibina.

**Resumen en una frase** – Una sola dosis guiada de psilocibina combinada con terapia ayudó a fumadores a dejar el tabaco seis veces más que los parches de nicotina en un nuevo ensayo clínico.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of two intertwined pathways—one made of glowing, vibrant mushrooms radiating light and life, the other formed by fading, cracked nicotine patches—winding through a serene, softly lit room where a peaceful figure silhouette reclines with closed eyes and gentle musical notes floating around, symbolizing transformation and healing through a guided, introspective journey.

articles.title: The best strength training plan might be simpler than you think
articles.summary: New strength training guidelines emphasize that doing any resistance training is what truly matters. Based on decades of research, experts say even simple routines can increase muscle, strength, and physical function. The key is not perfection but consistency. In fact, the best workout plan is the one you can maintain long term.
articles.content:
The first major update to resistance training recommendations in 17 years delivers a straightforward message. Even small amounts of resistance training can improve strength, increase muscle size, enhance power, and support overall physical function.

The updated guidance, released by the American College of Sports Medicine (ACSM) as a Position Stand, is built on 137 systematic reviews covering more than 30,000 participants. This makes it the most extensive and evidence-based set of resistance training recommendations to date.

"The best resistance training program is the one you'll actually stick with," says Stuart Phillips, distinguished professor in the Department of Kinesiology and an author on the Position Stand. "Training all major muscle groups at least twice a week matters far more than chasing the idea of a 'perfect' or complex training plan. Whether it's barbells, bands, or bodyweight, consistency and effort drive results."

Updated Guidance Reflects Surge in Strength Research

This update comes after years of growing scientific interest in muscle health and aging. The last ACSM Position Stand on resistance training for healthy adults was published in 2009, before a wave of new research on how strength impacts long term health and well-being.

"The new document reflects that surge in evidence and expands its recommendations to include more people and more types of training than ever before," Phillips says.

A key takeaway from the updated guidelines is that the biggest benefits often come from a simple starting point. Transitioning from no resistance training to any regular activity can lead to meaningful improvements. While factors such as load, volume, and frequency can be adjusted, experts say the main priority for most adults should be building a routine they can follow consistently.

No Gym Required for Strength and Muscle Gains

Another important shift in the recommendations is the recognition that effective resistance training does not require access to a gym. Exercises using elastic bands, bodyweight movements, or simple at home routines can still produce measurable gains in strength, muscle size, and daily function.

According to Phillips, strict rules about the "ideal" training plan are no longer supported by current evidence. Instead, personal preferences, enjoyment, and the ability to maintain a routine over time are what matter most. This approach is especially important for adults who want to stay strong, healthy, and capable as they age.

Focus on Consistency Over Complexity

Athletes and highly trained individuals may still need more specialized, sport specific programs. However, for most adults, the guidance is clear. Choose a resistance training routine that fits your lifestyle and stick with it over time.

The full ACSM Position Stand is now available in Medicine & Science in Sports & Exercise.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on updated, evidence-based strength training guidelines that emphasize the broad health benefits of simple, consistent resistance training accessible to most adults. This has a positive real-world impact by encouraging sustainable exercise habits that improve muscle health, physical function, and well-being, especially as people age. The guidance is significant and relevant to the general public, supported by extensive research, and clearly explained.
articles.personality_title: New guidelines show simple strength training works best
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The American College of Sports Medicine (ACSM) updated its strength training advice for the first time in 17 years. This update is based on a large review of studies involving over 30,000 people.

**What happened** – Experts released new guidelines saying that doing any kind of resistance training, even simple exercises at home without equipment, can improve muscle strength, size, and daily physical abilities. The key message is to train major muscle groups at least twice a week and focus on consistency rather than complicated routines.

**Impact** – This is important because it shows people don’t need a gym or perfect plan to get stronger and healthier. Even small, regular workouts can make a big difference, especially as adults get older. The guidelines encourage more people to start and keep up strength training by making it easy and flexible.

**What's next step** – People can now choose simple resistance exercises they enjoy and fit into their daily life. Researchers and trainers may promote these easy-to-follow routines to help more adults build lasting healthy habits.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The best strength training plan is one you can do regularly, even if it’s simple and done at home without special equipment.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les nouvelles recommandations montrent que la musculation simple est la meilleure
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le Collège américain de médecine sportive (ACSM) a mis à jour ses conseils pour la musculation pour la première fois en 17 ans, en s’appuyant sur une grande revue d’études avec plus de 30 000 participants.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Les experts ont publié de nouvelles recommandations indiquant que tout type d’entraînement en résistance, même des exercices simples à la maison sans équipement, peut améliorer la force musculaire, la taille des muscles et les capacités physiques quotidiennes. Le message principal est de travailler les principaux groupes musculaires au moins deux fois par semaine et de privilégier la régularité plutôt que des routines compliquées.

**Impact** – Cela est important car cela montre que les gens n’ont pas besoin d’une salle de sport ou d’un plan parfait pour devenir plus forts et en meilleure santé. Même de petits exercices réguliers peuvent faire une grande différence, surtout en vieillissant. Ces recommandations encouragent plus de personnes à commencer et à continuer la musculation en la rendant facile et flexible.

**Prochaine étape** – Les gens peuvent maintenant choisir des exercices simples qu’ils aiment et qui s’intègrent dans leur vie quotidienne. Les chercheurs et entraîneurs pourront promouvoir ces routines faciles à suivre pour aider davantage d’adultes à adopter des habitudes saines durables.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le meilleur programme de musculation est celui que vous pouvez faire régulièrement, même s’il est simple et réalisé à la maison sans équipement spécial.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevas pautas muestran que el entrenamiento de fuerza simple es el mejor
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El Colegio Americano de Medicina Deportiva (ACSM) actualizó sus recomendaciones para el entrenamiento de fuerza por primera vez en 17 años, basándose en una gran revisión de estudios con más de 30,000 participantes.

**Qué pasó** – Los expertos publicaron nuevas pautas que dicen que cualquier tipo de entrenamiento de resistencia, incluso ejercicios simples en casa sin equipo, puede mejorar la fuerza muscular, el tamaño del músculo y las habilidades físicas diarias. El mensaje clave es entrenar los principales grupos musculares al menos dos veces por semana y enfocarse en la constancia más que en rutinas complicadas.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque muestra que las personas no necesitan un gimnasio o un plan perfecto para volverse más fuertes y saludables. Incluso pequeños entrenamientos regulares pueden hacer una gran diferencia, especialmente a medida que las personas envejecen. Las pautas animan a más personas a comenzar y mantener el entrenamiento de fuerza haciéndolo fácil y flexible.

**Próximo paso** – Ahora las personas pueden elegir ejercicios simples que disfruten y que se adapten a su vida diaria. Los investigadores y entrenadores pueden promover estas rutinas fáciles de seguir para ayudar a más adultos a crear hábitos saludables duraderos.

**Resumen en una frase** – El mejor plan de entrenamiento de fuerza es el que puedes hacer regularmente, incluso si es simple y se realiza en casa sin equipo especial.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a cozy home interior featuring simple resistance training tools like elastic bands and a barbell resting beside a sturdy wooden chair, with soft natural light highlighting a pair of strong, abstract human silhouettes made of intertwining muscle-like vines, symbolizing growth and strength through consistent, accessible exercise.

articles.title: What happens after Ozempic shocked researchers
articles.summary: Stopping popular weight-loss injections like Ozempic or Mounjaro might not trigger the dramatic rebound many fear. A large real-world study of nearly 8,000 patients found that most people who discontinue these drugs manage to keep the weight off—or even continue losing—by restarting treatment, switching medications, or adopting lifestyle changes. While earlier clinical trials suggested rapid weight regain, this new evidence paints a more hopeful picture.
articles.content:
As injectable GLP-1 medications become more widely used, many people wonder what happens when patients stop taking them outside of controlled clinical trials.

A new analysis from Cleveland Clinic, involving nearly 8,000 patients, suggests that stopping drugs like semaglutide and tirzepatide does not typically result in major weight regain in real-world settings. Many patients either restart treatment later or switch to other weight management options, which may help limit weight gain.

In one of the largest real-world studies so far to examine long-term outcomes after discontinuing GLP-1 therapies, researchers found that a significant number of patients were able to maintain stable weight over a year. This was often achieved through alternative treatments and structured lifestyle support.

Real-World Data vs Clinical Trial Results

The findings, published in the journal Diabetes, Obesity and Metabolism, offer important context to earlier randomized clinical trials. Those trials showed that patients who stopped semaglutide (sold under the brand names Ozempic/Wegovy) and tirzepatide (sold under the brand names Mounjaro/Zepbound) regained more than half of the weight they had lost within a year.

The new data suggest that outcomes may differ in everyday clinical practice, where patients have more flexibility to adjust their treatment plans.

Hamlet Gasoyan, DS, Ph.D., MPH, a researcher with Cleveland Clinic's Center for Value-Based Care Research, led the study.

"Our real-world data show that many patients who stop semaglutide or tirzepatide restart the medication or transition to another obesity treatment, which may explain why they regain less weight than patients in randomized trials," Dr. Gasoyan said.

Study Details and Patient Outcomes

This retrospective cohort study included 7,938 adults with overweight or obesity in Ohio and Florida. All participants began treatment with injectable semaglutide or tirzepatide for either obesity or type 2 diabetes, then stopped using the medication within three to 12 months. Researchers tracked what treatments patients pursued afterward and how their weight changed over time.

Although most participants experienced meaningful weight loss, results varied across individuals:

Patients treated for obesity lost an average of 8.4% of their body weight before stopping the medication and regained an average of 0.5% after one year.

Patients treated for type 2 diabetes lost an average of 4.4% before discontinuation and went on to lose an additional 1.3% over the following year.

Among those treated for obesity, 55% gained weight after stopping, while 45% either continued losing weight or maintained their weight.

In the diabetes group, 44% gained weight, while 56% maintained or continued to lose weight.

Why Patients Stop and What They Do Next

Previous research by the same team identified two main reasons patients stop taking these medications: cost or lack of insurance coverage, and side effects. Financial barriers were the most common factor.

Patients using the drugs for type 2 diabetes were more likely to restart treatment compared with those using them for obesity. This difference appears to be linked to more consistent insurance coverage for diabetes prescriptions.

Within a year of stopping their initial GLP-1 medication, many patients explored other weight management strategies:

27% switched to another medication (including older-generation obesity drugs or switching between semaglutide and tirzepatide)

20% restarted their original medication

14% continued treatment through lifestyle-focused care with professionals such as dietitians or exercise specialists

Less than 1% underwent metabolic and bariatric surgery

Ongoing Support Key to Long-Term Weight Management

Researchers say these findings highlight the importance of continued, individualized care for people managing obesity, even if they stop medication.

"Many patients do not give up on their obesity treatment journey, even if they need to stop their initial medication," said Dr. Gasoyan. "In our future work, we will examine the comparative effectiveness of alternative treatment options for obesity in patients who discontinue semaglutide or tirzepatide, to help patients and their clinicians make informed decisions."
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a large real-world study showing that patients discontinuing popular weight-loss drugs like Ozempic and Mounjaro often maintain or continue to lose weight through alternative treatments or lifestyle changes, challenging previous clinical trial concerns about rapid weight regain. This has broad significance for obesity and diabetes management, offering hope and practical insights for millions affected by these conditions.
articles.personality_title: Study finds many keep weight off after stopping Ozempic or Mounjaro
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Injectable drugs like Ozempic and Mounjaro, used to help with weight loss and diabetes, have become popular. But there was worry that stopping these medicines would cause people to quickly gain back the weight they lost.

**What happened** – Researchers at Cleveland Clinic studied nearly 8,000 adults who stopped taking these drugs after using them for a few months. They found that most people either kept their weight steady or even continued to lose weight by restarting treatment, switching medicines, or improving their diet and exercise routines.

**Impact** – This study is important because it shows real-life results are different from earlier clinical trials, which suggested people would regain a lot of weight after stopping. Instead, many patients managed their weight well, often with new treatments or lifestyle help. This gives a clearer picture of what really happens when people stop these drugs.

**What's next step** – The researchers plan to study which alternative treatments work best for people who stop these medicines. This will help doctors and patients make better choices for long-term weight management.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Many patients successfully maintain or lose weight after stopping Ozempic or Mounjaro by using other treatments or lifestyle changes, showing that quitting these drugs doesn’t always mean quick weight regain.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une étude montre que beaucoup gardent leur poids après avoir arrêté Ozempic ou Mounjaro
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les médicaments injectables comme Ozempic et Mounjaro, utilisés pour perdre du poids et traiter le diabète, sont très répandus. Mais on craignait que l’arrêt de ces médicaments entraîne une reprise rapide du poids perdu.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de la Cleveland Clinic ont étudié près de 8 000 adultes ayant arrêté ces médicaments après quelques mois d’utilisation. Ils ont découvert que la plupart des personnes ont soit stabilisé leur poids, soit continué à perdre du poids en reprenant le traitement, en changeant de médicament ou en améliorant leur alimentation et leur activité physique.

**Impact** – Cette étude est importante car elle montre que les résultats dans la vie réelle diffèrent des essais cliniques précédents, qui suggéraient une reprise importante du poids après l’arrêt. Au contraire, beaucoup de patients gèrent bien leur poids, souvent grâce à de nouveaux traitements ou un accompagnement sur le mode de vie. Cela donne une image plus claire de ce qui se passe vraiment après l’arrêt de ces médicaments.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont étudier quels traitements alternatifs sont les plus efficaces pour les personnes qui arrêtent ces médicaments. Cela aidera médecins et patients à faire de meilleurs choix pour la gestion du poids à long terme.

**Résumé en une phrase** – De nombreux patients réussissent à maintenir ou à perdre du poids après avoir arrêté Ozempic ou Mounjaro, grâce à d’autres traitements ou changements de mode de vie, ce qui montre que l’arrêt de ces médicaments ne signifie pas toujours une reprise rapide du poids.
articles.personality_title_es: Estudio muestra que muchos mantienen el peso tras dejar Ozempic o Mounjaro
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los medicamentos inyectables como Ozempic y Mounjaro, usados para perder peso y tratar la diabetes, se han vuelto muy populares. Pero existía preocupación de que al dejar estos medicamentos las personas recuperaran rápido el peso perdido.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de Cleveland Clinic estudiaron a casi 8,000 adultos que dejaron de usar estos medicamentos después de algunos meses. Encontraron que la mayoría mantuvo su peso o incluso siguió perdiendo peso al reiniciar el tratamiento, cambiar de medicamento o mejorar su dieta y ejercicio.

**Impacto** – Este estudio es importante porque muestra que los resultados en la vida real son diferentes a los de ensayos clínicos anteriores, que indicaban una rápida recuperación del peso tras dejar el medicamento. En cambio, muchos pacientes manejaron bien su peso, a menudo con nuevos tratamientos o apoyo para cambiar su estilo de vida. Esto da una imagen más clara de lo que sucede realmente cuando se dejan estos medicamentos.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean estudiar qué tratamientos alternativos funcionan mejor para quienes dejan estos medicamentos. Esto ayudará a médicos y pacientes a tomar mejores decisiones para el manejo del peso a largo plazo.

**Resumen en una frase** – Muchos pacientes logran mantener o perder peso tras dejar Ozempic o Mounjaro usando otros tratamientos o cambios en su estilo de vida, mostrando que dejar estos medicamentos no siempre significa recuperar peso rápidamente.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a winding path through a calm landscape, where symbolic medicine bottles shaped like gentle hills transition smoothly into open hands releasing small, balanced scales, representing ongoing care and weight management, all rendered in soft, natural earth tones.

articles.title: Masked mitochondria slip into cells to treat disease in mice
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 19 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00869-2Transplantation technique lengthened survival in animals with an often-fatal mitochondrial disease.
articles.content:
Mitochondria (artist’s impression) wrapped in red-blood-cell membranes can sneak into cells without being tagged for destruction.Credit: Alfred Pasieka/SPL

A well-fitted ‘disguise’ allows transplanted mitochondria to slip into cells whose own mitochondria are defective, scientists reported 18 March in Cell1. Administration of these cloaked mitochondria prolonged the life of mice with a deadly disease caused by abnormal mitochondria.

The scientists found that a mitochondrion wrapped in the membrane of a red blood cell can enter a cell without triggering protective mechanisms that would typically destroy the organelle. The technique “hugely” increased the efficiency of the treatment compared with previous methods, says Mike Devine, a neurobiologist at the Francis Crick Institute in London, who was not involved in the study. The difference is like “night and day”, he says.

But scientists also expressed scepticism about some aspects of the study. The work is a “remarkable advance”, but the conclusion that the method prevents Parkinson’s disease in a mouse model is “overstated,” says Ken Nakamura, a neuroscientist at the Gladstone Institutes in San Francisco, California.

Targeted for destruction

Mitochondria are cellular substructures that produce fuel to power cellular activity. They have their own genomes, and mutations in their DNA cause diseases such as Leigh syndrome, a rare and often fatal disorder that usually strikes during early childhood.

Cancer cells get power boost by stealing mitochondria from nerves

Scientists have long sought ways to transplant normal mitochondria into cells. But when mitochondria are exposed to tissue or blood, they lose the electrical gradient across their outer membrane. Mitochondria that lack such a gradient are recognized by a cell’s internal machinery as damaged and quickly destroyed2.

The vast majority of previous studies involved injecting “naked” mitochondria directly into the bloodstream or tissue sites, says Noa Sher, chief scientific officer at Minovia Therapeutics in Haifa, Israel. But the approach isn’t very efficient, so researchers often have to use “ridiculous” doses of mitochondria that would be infeasible when scaled to a human-sized organism. “It is immediately obvious that you should protect the mitochondria if they’re going to be outside of the cell,” Sher says. “What to put them in is more complicated.”

Shrink-wrapped

The authors of the Cell study settled on using the membranes of red blood cells because the cells lack organelles with their own membranes. Creating the mitochondrial ‘capsules’ was then as simple as mixing ruptured red blood cells and mitochondria isolated using a commercially available kit. The scientists then injected these ‘capsules’ into mice.

The “shell” preserves the mitochondrion’s electrical gradient, says co-author Qi Long, a biologist at Guangzhou Medical University in China. That allowed the organelles to slip into recipient cells undetected.

A ‘capsule’ of mitochondria (central dark circle) is enclosed by a red blood cell’s membrane (outer dark oval).Credit: S. Du et al./Cell

Using previous methods, less than 5% of cells growing in laboratory dishes absorbed the mitochondria, says co-author Xingguo Liu, a biologist at the Guangzhou Institutes of Biomedicine and Health in China. “Our efficiency is super high,” he says. “It’s around 80%.”

In a mouse model of Leigh syndrome, capsules of mitochondria increased survival of the mice by about two weeks. That’s about 20% longer than mice treated with free-floating mitochondria.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in mitochondrial transplantation that substantially improves treatment efficiency and prolongs survival in mice with a fatal mitochondrial disease. This advancement has broad implications for treating mitochondrial disorders, which affect many people, and provides a hopeful step toward better therapies.
articles.personality_title: New technique helps mitochondria enter cells and extend life in sick mice
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Mitochondria are tiny parts inside cells that produce energy. When mitochondria have problems, they can cause serious diseases like Leigh syndrome, which often affects children and can be deadly. Scientists have tried to fix these diseases by putting healthy mitochondria into cells, but the mitochondria usually get destroyed before they can help.

**What happened** – Researchers found a way to protect mitochondria by wrapping them in membranes taken from red blood cells. This disguise helps the mitochondria slip into cells without being destroyed. They tested this method on mice with Leigh syndrome and found that the treated mice lived about 20% longer than those given unprotected mitochondria.

**Impact** – This method is much more efficient than earlier attempts. While previous methods only got mitochondria into about 5% of cells, this new approach reached around 80%. This shows a big improvement in delivering healthy mitochondria into cells, which could help treat mitochondrial diseases more effectively in the future.

**What's next step** – Scientists will likely continue to test this technique to see if it works well in other diseases and in larger animals. They also need to confirm if it can be safe and effective for humans. More research will help understand how to best use this method for treatments.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Wrapping mitochondria in red blood cell membranes helps them enter cells better and extends life in mice with a deadly mitochondrial disease.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle technique aide les mitochondries à entrer dans les cellules et à prolonger la vie des souris malades
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les mitochondries sont de petites parties à l'intérieur des cellules qui produisent de l'énergie. Lorsque les mitochondries ont un problème, elles peuvent causer des maladies graves comme le syndrome de Leigh, qui touche souvent les enfants et peut être mortel. Les scientifiques ont essayé de réparer ces maladies en mettant des mitochondries saines dans les cellules, mais elles sont généralement détruites avant de pouvoir aider.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs ont trouvé un moyen de protéger les mitochondries en les enveloppant dans des membranes prélevées sur des globules rouges. Ce déguisement permet aux mitochondries de pénétrer dans les cellules sans être détruites. Ils ont testé cette méthode sur des souris atteintes du syndrome de Leigh et ont constaté que les souris traitées vivaient environ 20 % plus longtemps que celles recevant des mitochondries non protégées.

**Impact** – Cette méthode est beaucoup plus efficace que les tentatives précédentes. Alors que les méthodes anciennes ne permettaient d'introduire des mitochondries que dans environ 5 % des cellules, cette nouvelle approche atteint environ 80 %. Cela montre une grande amélioration pour apporter des mitochondries saines dans les cellules, ce qui pourrait aider à mieux traiter les maladies mitochondriales à l'avenir.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont probablement continuer à tester cette technique pour voir si elle fonctionne bien pour d'autres maladies et chez des animaux plus grands. Ils doivent aussi vérifier si elle est sûre et efficace chez l'humain. Plus de recherches permettront de mieux comprendre comment utiliser cette méthode pour les traitements.

**Résumé en une phrase** – En enveloppant les mitochondries dans des membranes de globules rouges, on améliore leur entrée dans les cellules et on prolonge la vie des souris atteintes d'une maladie mitochondriale mortelle.
articles.personality_title_es: Nueva técnica ayuda a las mitocondrias a entrar en células y prolonga la vida de ratones enfermos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las mitocondrias son pequeñas partes dentro de las células que producen energía. Cuando las mitocondrias tienen problemas, pueden causar enfermedades graves como el síndrome de Leigh, que suele afectar a niños y puede ser mortal. Los científicos han intentado reparar estas enfermedades poniendo mitocondrias sanas dentro de las células, pero normalmente las mitocondrias son destruidas antes de poder ayudar.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores encontraron una manera de proteger las mitocondrias envolviéndolas en membranas tomadas de glóbulos rojos. Este disfraz ayuda a las mitocondrias a entrar en las células sin ser destruidas. Probaron este método en ratones con síndrome de Leigh y descubrieron que los ratones tratados vivían aproximadamente un 20 % más que los que recibieron mitocondrias sin protección.

**Impacto** – Este método es mucho más eficiente que los intentos anteriores. Mientras que los métodos previos lograban que las mitocondrias entraran en solo el 5 % de las células, este nuevo método alcanza alrededor del 80 %. Esto muestra una gran mejora para entregar mitocondrias sanas a las células, lo que podría ayudar a tratar mejor las enfermedades mitocondriales en el futuro.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos probablemente continuarán probando esta técnica para ver si funciona bien en otras enfermedades y en animales más grandes. También necesitan confirmar si es segura y efectiva para humanos. Más investigaciones ayudarán a entender mejor cómo usar este método para tratamientos.

**Resumen en una frase** – Envolver las mitocondrias en membranas de glóbulos rojos les ayuda a entrar mejor en las células y prolonga la vida de ratones con una enfermedad mitocondrial mortal.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a glowing, intricately textured mitochondrion gently enveloped by a translucent red blood cell membrane, floating serenely as it slips through a softly illuminated cellular landscape symbolized by abstract, softly rounded cell shapes, conveying protection and seamless integration.
